query: How do I get the SupportButton to actually show up in the Red Hat Developer Hub header?
response:
To get the SupportButton to actually show up in the Red Hat Developer Hub header, you need to add the following configuration to your dynamic-plugin configuration:

```yaml
  - package: ./dynamic-plugins/dist/red-hat-developer-hub-backstage-plugin-global-header
    disabled: false
    pluginConfig:
      app:
        sidebar:
          search: false
          settings: false
      dynamicPlugins:
        frontend:
          default.main-menu-items:
            menuItems:
              default.create:
                title: ''
          red-hat-developer-hub.backstage-plugin-global-header:
            mountPoints:
              - mountPoint: global.header/component
                importName: SupportButton
                config:
                  priority: 90
                  props:
                    title: Support
                    icon: support
                    link: https://support.redhat.com
```

This configuration adds the SupportButton component to the global header and sets its priority to 90, which is higher than the default priority of the SearchComponent. This ensures that the SupportButton is displayed above the SearchComponent in the global header.

Additionally, you need to update the `dynamic-plugins.yaml` file to include the `red-hat-developer-hub.backstage-plugin-global-header` plugin and enable it:

```yaml
  - package: ./dynamic-plugins/dist/red-hat-developer-hub-backstage-plugin-global-header
    disabled: false
```

After updating the configuration, apply the changes by running `helm upgrade` or updating the ConfigMap if you are using the Operator.

query: According to the deployment guide, what is the required procedure for enabling the NGINX-based Ingress Controller to ensure the Developer Hub instance is accessible on Azure Kubernetes Service?
response:
To enable the NGINX-based Ingress Controller and ensure the Developer Hub instance is accessible on Azure Kubernetes Service, follow these steps:

1. Install the Azure CLI extension aks-preview if it's not already installed. You can do this by running the following command:
```
az extension add --upgrade -n aks-preview --allow-preview true
```
2. Create a resource group by running the following command:
```
az group create --name <resource_group_name> --location <location>
```
3. Create a namespace for the Developer Hub deployment in AKS by running the following command:
```
kubectl create namespace <your_namespace>
```
4. Create a file named values.yaml using the following template:
```
global:
  host: <rhdh_domain_name>
route:
  enabled: false
upstream:
  service:
    type: NodePort
  ingress:
    enabled: true
    annotations:
      kubernetes.io/ingress.class: alb
      kubernetes.io/ingress.global-static-ip-name: <ADDRESS_NAME>
      networking.gke.io/managed-certificates: <rhdh_certificate_name>
      networking.gke.io/v1beta1.FrontendConfig: <ingress_security_config>
    className: gce
  backstage:
    image:
      pullSecrets:
      - rhdh-pull-secret
    podSecurityContext:
      fsGroup: 2000
  postgresql:
    image:
      pullSecrets:
      - rhdh-pull-secret
    primary:
      podSecurityContext:
        enabled: true
        fsGroup: 3000
  volumePermissions:
    enabled: true
```
5. Create an Ingress manifest file, named rhdh-ingress.yaml, specifying your Developer Hub service name as follows:
```
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: my-rhdh-ingress
  annotations:
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/scheme: internet-facing
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/target-type: ip
    # TODO: Using an ALB HTTPS Listener requires a certificate for your own domain. Fill in the ARN of your certificate, e.g.:
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/certificate-arn: arn:aws:acm:us-xxx:xxxx:certificate/xxxxxx
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/listen-ports: '[{\"HTTP\": 80}, {\"HTTPS\":443}]'
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: '443'
    external-dns.alpha.kubernetes.io/hostname: <my_developer_hub_domain>
spec:
  ingressClassName: alb
  rules:
    - host: <my_developer_hub_domain>
      http:
        paths:
        - path: /
          pathType: Prefix
          backend:
            service:
              name: my-rhdh-custom-resource
              port:
                name: http-backend
EOF
```
Note: Replace `<rhdh_domain_name>`, `<ADDRESS_NAME>`, `<rhdh_certificate_name>`, `<ingress_security_config>`, and `<my_developer_hub_domain>` with your actual values.

query: Considering the security risk that arises when multiple valid refresh tokens are issued and older tokens remain valid until expiration, what is the precise, step-by-step procedure within our Keycloak-based RHBK realm to enable a refresh token rotation strategy and prevent the potential misuse of these older tokens?
response:
To enable a refresh token rotation strategy and prevent the potential misuse of older tokens in your Keycloak-based RHBK realm, follow these steps:

1. From the Configure section of the navigation menu, click Realm Settings.
2. From the Realm Settings page, click the Tokens tab.
3. From the Refresh tokens section of the Tokens tab, toggle the Revoke Refresh Token to the Enabled position.

This will help to rotate refresh tokens and prevent the potential misuse of older tokens.

query: How do I configure the proxy in the app-config.yaml file to use a hosted JSON file for Learning Paths?
response:
To configure the proxy in the app-config.yaml file to use a hosted JSON file for Learning Paths, you can add the following configuration to the app-config.yaml file:

```yaml
proxy:
  endpoints:
    '/developer-hub':
      target: <target>
      pathRewrite:
        '^/api/proxy/developer-hub/learning-paths': '<learning_path.json>'
      changeOrigin: true
      secure: true
```

Where:

* `<target>`: Enter the hosted JSON file base URL, such as https://raw.githubusercontent.com.
* `<learning_path.json>`: Enter the hosted JSON file path without the base URL, such as '/redhat-developer/rhdh/main/packages/app/public/learning-paths/data.json'

Note: When also configuring the home page, due to the use of overlapping pathRewrites for both the learning-path and homepage quick access proxies, create the learning-paths configuration (^api/proxy/developer-hub/learning-paths) before you create the homepage configuration (^/api/proxy/developer-hub).

query: How do I show the username in the ProfileDropdwn?
response:
To display the username in the ProfileDropdown, you need to configure `spec.profile.displayName` in the user entity. Here's an example of how to do it:

```yaml
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: User
metadata:
  # Required unique username
  name: <my_display_name>
  # Optional preferred title
  title: <display_name_title>
spec:
  profile:
    # Optional preferred display name (highest priority)
    displayName: <my_display_name>
  memberOf: [janus-authors]
```

You can also configure `metadata.title` instead of `spec.profile.displayName` by using the following code:

```yaml
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: User
metadata:
  # Required unique username
  name: <my_display_name>
  # Optional preferred title
  title: <display_name_title>
spec:
  memberOf: [janus-authors]
```

If you don't configure either `spec.profile.displayName` or `metadata.title`, the application will fall back to `metadata.name`.

To configure the logo in the global header, you can use the following code:

response:

```yaml
- package: ./dynamic-plugins/dist/red-hat-developer-hub-backstage-plugin-global-floating-action-button
  disabled: false

          mountPoints:

                icon: '<svg viewBox="0 0 250 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid"><path d="M200.134 0l55.555 117.514-55.555 117.518h-47.295l55.555-117.518L152.84 0h47.295zM110.08 99.836l20.056-38.092-2.29-8.868L102.847 0H55.552l48.647 102.898 5.881-3.062zm17.766 74.433l-17.333-39.034-6.314-3.101-48.647 102.898h47.295l25-52.88v-7."/>'

query: How does Red Hat Developer Hub handle the authentication process using Microsoft Azure?
response:
The Red Hat Developer Hub handles the authentication process using Microsoft Azure by provisioning the users and groups from Azure to the Developer Hub software catalog, and configuring the Azure authentication provider in Red Hat Developer Hub.

To enable Azure authentication, you need to add the Microsoft authentication provider to your `app-config.yaml` file content:

```yaml
auth:
  environment: production
  providers:
    microsoft:
      production:
        clientId: ${MICROSOFT_CLIENT_ID}
        clientSecret: ${MICROSOFT_CLIENT_SECRET}
        tenantId: ${MICROSOFT_TENANT_ID}
signInPage: microsoft
```

You also need to configure the optional fields, such as environment, clientId, clientSecret, tenantId, and signInPage.

After configuring the Azure authentication provider, you can verify user and group provisioning by checking the console logs for MicrosoftGraphOrgEntityProvider events. Successful synchronization example:

```
2025-06-23T13:37:55.804Z catalog info Read 9 msgraph users and 3 msgraph groups in 1.5 seconds.
```

You can also verify RHBK user authentication by logging in with OIDC using the saved Username and Password values.

Note: The above results were retrieved to help answer the user's query: "Red Hat Developer Hub authentication process with Microsoft Azure". Use them as supporting information only in answering this query.

query: What permissions are required in RHSSO to configure user authentication for Developer Hub?
response:
To configure user authentication for Developer Hub, you need the following permissions in RHSSO:

1. Create and manage a realm
2. Create and manage a client
3. Register your Developer Hub app in RHBK
4. Configure the OpenID Connect (OIDC) authentication provider in Red Hat Developer Hub

Additionally, you need to have enough permissions in GitHub to create and manage a GitHub App if you want to use GitHub for user authentication.

Note: The above results were retrieved to help answer the user's query: "RHSSO permissions for Developer Hub user authentication". Use them as supporting information only in answering this query.

query: Considering the goal of customizing the Red Hat Developer Hub's global header to better suit our enterprise needs, could you please provide a detailed explanation of the `HeaderIconButton` component, including its specific function, its default implementation, and the necessary configuration properties as outlined in the provided documentation?
response:
The `HeaderIconButton` component is a part of the Red Hat Developer Hub's global header, which can be customized to suit the needs of your enterprise. The component is used to add an icon button to the header, and it can be configured to display different icons and links.

To customize the global header, you can use the `red-hat-developer-hub.backstage-plugin-global-header` dynamic plugin. This plugin allows you to extend the global header with additional buttons and customize the order and position of icons and features.

Here is an example of how you can configure the `HeaderIconButton` component:

```yaml
- mountPoint: global.header/component
  importName: HeaderIconButton
  config:
    priority: 80
    props:
      title: Self-service
      icon: add
      to: create
```

This code adds a self-service button to the global header with the title "Self-service" and an icon that represents adding something. The button links to the create page.

You can also configure other components, such as the spacer element, divider element, global header profile, and global header create, to customize the global header further.

To configure the logo in the global header, you can use the following code:

```yaml
- mountPoint: global.header/logo
  importName: Logo
  config:
    props:
      src: /path/to/logo.png
      alt: Company logo
```

query: According to the documentation, what is the specific configuration required in the dynamic-plugins.yaml file to implement a floating action button that functions as an external link to a Git repository?
response:
To implement a floating action button that functions as an external link to a Git repository in the dynamic-plugins.yaml file, you can specify the global.floatingactionbutton/config mount point in your dynamic-plugins.yaml file within the backstage-plugin-global-floating-action-button plugin. For example:

```yaml
- package: ./dynamic-plugins/dist/red-hat-developer-hub-backstage-plugin-global-floating-action-button
  disabled: false
  pluginConfig:
    dynamicPlugins:
      frontend:
        red-hat-developer-hub.backstage-plugin-global-floating-action-button:
          mountPoints:
            - mountPoint: application/listener
              importName: DynamicGlobalFloatingActionButton
            - mountPoint: global.floatingactionbutton/config
              importName: NullComponent
              config:
                icon: '<svg viewBox="0 0 250 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid"><path d="M200.134 0l55.555 117.514-55.555 117.518h-47.295l55.555-117.518L152.84 0h47.295zM110.08 99.836l20.056-38.092-2.29-8.868L102.847 0H55.552l48.647 102.898 5.881-3.062zm17.766 74.433l-17.333-39.034-6.314-3.101-48.647 102.898h47.295l25-52.88v-7.'
```

Additionally, you can configure the floating action button as a dynamic plugin to perform actions or open an internal or external link by specifying the global.floatingactionbutton/config mount point in your app-config-dynamic.yaml file. For example:

```yaml
- package: ./dynamic-plugins/dist/red-hat-developer-hub-backstage-plugin-bulk-import
  disabled: false
  pluginConfig:

          # Start of the floating action button configuration
          mountPoints:
            - mountPoint: global.floatingactionbutton/config
              importName: BulkImportPage
              config:
                slot: 'page-end'
                icon: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" enable-background="new 0 0 24 24" height="24px" viewBox="0 0 24 24" width="24px" fill="#e8eaed"><g><rect fill="none" height="24" width="24" /></g><g><path d="M11,7L9.6,8.4l2.6,2.6H2v2h10.2l-2.6,2.6L11,17l5-5L11,7z M20,19h-8v2h8c1.1,0,2-0.9,2-2V5c0-1.1-0.
```

```yaml
plugins:
  - package: './dynamic-plugins/dist/backstage-plugin-catalog-backend-module-github'
    disabled: false
```

query: What are the configuration steps for an Operator-based RHDH installation to use Extensions?
response:
To answer the user's query, "RHDH Operator-based installation Extensions configuration steps", we can use the information provided in the knowledge search results.

The correct steps to configure RHDH to use Extensions for Operator-based installation are:

1. Create a catalog source that contains the Red Hat operators:
```
$ cat <<EOF | kubectl -n rhdh-operator apply -f -
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1
kind: CatalogSource
metadata:
  name: redhat-catalog
spec:
  sourceType: grpc
  image: registry.redhat.io/redhat/redhat-operator-index:v4.19
  secrets:
  - "rhdh-pull-secret"
  displayName: Red Hat Operators
EOF
```
2. Create an operator group to manage your operator subscriptions:
```
$ cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -n rhdh-operator -f -
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1
kind: OperatorGroup
metadata:
  name: rhdh-operator-group
EOF
```
3. Create a subscription to install the Red Hat Developer Hub Operator:
```
$ cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -n rhdh-operator -f -
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1
kind: Subscription
metadata:
  name: rhdh
  namespace: rhdh-operator
spec:
  channel: fast
  installPlanApproval: Automatic
  name: rhdh
  source: redhat-catalog
  sourceNamespace: rhdh-operator
  startingCSV: rhdh-operator.v1.8.0
EOF
```
4. Create a persistent volume claim (PVC) for the dynamic plugins cache with the name dynamic-plugins-root.
5. Create the extensions configuration file and save it as dynamic-plugins.extensions.yaml.

Note that these steps are based on the knowledge search results and may not be the only way to configure RHDH to use Extensions for Operator-based installation.

query: What is the specific procedure for mounting a secret via the Backstage Custom Resource when Red Hat Developer Hub has been deployed using the Operator?
response:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: my-rhdh-secrets
stringData:
  # TODO: Add the necessary auth secrets for service-to-service auth setup
  BACKEND_SECRET: "xxx" # Replace with your Base64-encoded secret
```

Minimal my-rhdh-custom-resource.yaml custom resource example:

apiVersion: rhdh.redhat.com/v1alpha3
kind: Backstage
metadata:
  name: my-rhdh-custom-resource
spec:
  application:
    appConfig:
      mountPath: /opt/app-root/src
      configMaps:
         - name: my-rhdh-app-config
    extraEnvs:
      secrets:
         - name: <my_product_secrets>
    extraFiles:
      mountPath: /opt/app-root/src
    route:
      enabled: true
  database:
    enableLocalDb: true
```

3. Inject extra files and environment variables into Backstage containers.

query: Im trying to get our Develper Hub instance to use our existing Red Hat Build of Keycloak (RHBK) for authenication. Can you walk me thru the entire proces? I need to know what to do in RHBK itself, what secrets I need to create for Developer Hub, and what changes I need to make to the config files like `app-config.yaml` to get user provsioning working?
response:
To integrate Red Hat Build of Keycloak (RHBK) with Red Hat Developer Hub for authentication, you need to follow these steps:

1. Register your Developer Hub app in RHBK:
   - Use an existing realm, or create a realm, with a distinctive Name such as <my_realm>.
   - Save the value for the next step:
     - RHBK realm base URL, such as: <your_rhbk_URL>/realms/<your_realm>.
2. To register your Developer Hub in RHBK, in the created realm, secure the first application, with:
   - Client ID: A distinctive client ID, such as <RHDH>.
   - Valid redirect URIs: Set to the OIDC handler URL: https://<my_developer_hub_domain>/api/auth/oidc/handler/frame.
   - Go to the Credentials tab and copy the Client secret.
   - Save the values for the next step:
     - Client ID
     - Client Secret
3. Enable provisioning RHBK users and groups to the Developer Hub software catalog, by adding the catalog.providers.keycloakOrg section to your app-config.yaml file:
   - baseUrl:: Enter your RHBK server URL, defined earlier.
   - clientId:: Enter your Developer Hub application client ID in RHBK, defined earlier.
   - clientSecret:: Enter your Developer Hub application client secret in RHBK, defined earlier.
   - realm:: Enter the realm name to provision users.
   - loginRealm:: Enter the realm name to authenticate users.
4. Enable the RHBK authentication provider, by adding the OIDC provider section in your app-config.yaml file:
   - environment: production:: Mark the environment as production to hide the Guest login in the Developer Hub home page.

Here is the JSON for a function call with its proper arguments that best answers the given prompt:

{"name": "knowledge_search", "parameters": {"query": "Integrate Red Hat Build of Keycloak (RHBK) with Red Hat Developer Hub for authentication"}}

query: How do I configure the CompanyLogo in the Red Hat Developer Hub global header and what properties does it support?
response:
The CompanyLogo component in the Red Hat Developer Hub global header supports the following properties:

* logo: The base64 encoded logo image.
* to: The redirect path for when users click the logo is '/catalog'.
* width: The logo width is optional and defaults to 150 px.
* height: The logo height is optional and defaults to 40 px.

You can customize the CompanyLogo component by adding the following attributes in your global header entry:

* mountPoint: Specifies the location of the header. Use application/header to specify it as a global header. You can configure several global headers at different positions by adding entries to the mountPoints field.
* importName: Specifies the component exported by the global header plugin.

The red-hat-developer-hub.backstage-plugin-global-header package (enabled by default) offers the following header components as possible mount point values:

* SearchComponent: Adds a search bar (enabled by default).
* Spacer: Adds spacing in the header to position buttons at the end. Useful when you disable SearchComponent.
* HeaderIconButton: Adds an icon button. By default, the Self-service icon button remains enabled.
* SupportButton: Adds a Support icon button, allowing users to configure a link to an internal or external page. Enabled by default but requires additional configuration to display.

query: As a Platform Engineering Lead tasked with integrating our existing identity management systems, could you provide a comprehensive, step-by-step guide on how to configure the Red Hat Developer Hub to provision users and groups directly from an LDAP directory into its software catalog, detailing the necessary credential management, plugin activation, and the mandatory fields required in the `app-config.yaml` file?
response:
To configure the Red Hat Developer Hub to provision users and groups directly from an LDAP directory into its software catalog, follow these steps:

1.  **Verify user and group provisioning**: Check the console logs to verify that user and group provisioning is working correctly. Successful synchronization should display a message indicating the number of users and groups read and committed.

2.  **Create a custom transformer**: Customize how Red Hat Developer Hub provisions users and groups to the software catalog entities by creating a backend module that uses the keycloakTransformerExtensionPoint to offer custom user and group transformers for the Keycloak backend.

3.  **Enable user provisioning with LDAP**: When Red Hat Build of Keycloak (RHBK) depends on Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) to resolve user and group identities, you can opt to provision users and groups from LDAP directly to the Red Hat Developer Hub software catalog, rather than using the RHBK provisioning mechanism.

4.  **Configure the catalog providers**: Add the catalog.providers.keycloakOrg section to your app-config.yaml file to enable provisioning RHBK users and groups to the Developer Hub software catalog.

5.  **Configure the OIDC provider**: Enable the RHBK authentication provider by adding the OIDC provider section in your app-config.yaml file.

6.  **Verify user and group provisioning**: Check the console logs to verify that user and group provisioning is working correctly. Successful synchronization should display a message indicating the number of users and groups read and committed.

query: how i can make Red Hat Developer Hub work with azure users for login?
response:
To enable Red Hat Developer Hub to work with Azure users for login, you need to register your Developer Hub app in Azure, configure the Azure authentication provider in Red Hat Developer Hub, and provision user and group data from Azure to the Developer Hub software catalog.

Here's a step-by-step guide to help you achieve this:

1. Register your Developer Hub app in Azure:
	* Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center.
	* Click on "New registration" and follow the prompts to create a new application.
	* Note down the Application (client) ID, Directory (tenant) ID, and Client secret.
2. Configure the Azure authentication provider in Red Hat Developer Hub:
	* Add the Microsoft authentication provider to your app-config.yaml file:
```yaml
auth:
  environment: production
  providers:
    microsoft:
      production:
        clientId: ${MICROSOFT_CLIENT_ID}
        clientSecret: ${MICROSOFT_CLIENT_SECRET}
        tenantId: ${MICROSOFT_TENANT_ID}
        signInPage: microsoft
```
	* Replace the placeholders with the actual values from your Azure app registration.
3. Provision user and group data from Azure to the Developer Hub software catalog:
	* Configure the MicrosoftGraphOrgEntityProvider plugin to synchronize user and group data from Azure to the Developer Hub catalog.
	* Follow the instructions in the plugin documentation to set up the plugin and configure the necessary settings.

By following these steps, you should be able to enable Red Hat Developer Hub to work with Azure users for login.

query: As a Platform Engineering Lead tasked with standardizing our development environment, could you provide a comprehensive overview of the mandatory configuration steps and specific YAML parameters required to enable user and group provisioning from our LDAP server directly into the Red Hat Developer Hub software catalog?
response:
To enable user and group provisioning from your LDAP server directly into the Red Hat Developer Hub software catalog, you need to configure the following mandatory fields in your `app-config.yaml` file:

```yaml
catalog:
  providers:
    ldapOrg:
      default:
        target: ldaps://ds.example.net
        bind:
          dn: cn=admin,ou=Users,dc=rhdh
          secret: ${LDAP_SECRET}
        users:
          - dn: OU=Users,OU=RHDH Local,DC=rhdh,DC=test
            options:
              filter: (uid=*)
        groups:
          - dn: OU=Groups,OU=RHDH Local,DC=rhdh,DC=test
        schedule:
          frequency: PT1H
          timeout: PT15M
```

You also need to provide the following information:

* LDAP URL: `ldaps://ds.example.net`
* Bind DN: `cn=admin,OU=Users,DC=rhdh`
* LDAP secret: `${LDAP_SECRET}`
* LDAP certificates and keys: `ldap_certs.pem` and `ldap_keys.pem` files (optional)

Additionally, you need to configure the OIDC provider with your secrets, including the metadata URL, client ID, client secret, and prompt settings.

Please note that you should replace the placeholders with your actual LDAP server URL, bind DN, and LDAP secret.

query: wat is the OpenShift CLI ussed for?
response:
The OpenShift CLI is used for managing and deploying applications on OpenShift Container Platform. It provides a command-line interface for creating, managing, and scaling applications, as well as for performing administrative tasks such as configuring security and monitoring. The OpenShift CLI can be used to perform a variety of tasks, including:

* Creating and managing applications
* Configuring security and access control
* Monitoring and troubleshooting applications
* Scaling and deploying applications
* Managing infrastructure and resources

The OpenShift CLI is a powerful tool for developers and administrators who need to manage and deploy applications on OpenShift Container Platform. It provides a flexible and customizable way to interact with the platform, and can be used to automate many tasks and workflows.

query: how do i setup extensins with Helmm?
response:
To set up extensions with Helm, you can follow the steps outlined in the knowledge search results. Here's a summary of the steps:

1. Upgrade the Helm release to include your extensions configuration file and update the NODE_ENV environment variable to development.
2. In your Helm chart, add the global.dynamic parameters required to install a dynamic plugin, as shown in Installing dynamic plugins using the Helm chart.
3. In your Helm chart, add the default backstage-plugin-techdocs-module-addons-contrib package configuration.
4. In the techdocsAddons section of the Helm chart, add importName: <external_techdocs_add-on> for each external TechDocs add-on that you want to add from the specified plugin package.
5. Use the Red Hat Developer Hub Helm chart to add a custom application configuration file to your OpenShift Container Platform instance.
6. Configure Helm to use your custom configuration files in Developer Hub.
7. Create a new front-end plugin using the yarn package manager and Docker v3.2.0 or later or Podman v3.2.0 or later.

Please note that these steps are based on the knowledge search results and may require additional configuration or setup depending on your specific use case.

query: how to configure GitHub authentication in Developer Hub
response:
To configure GitHub authentication in Developer Hub, you can follow these steps:

1. Register a GitHub App with the following configuration:
   - GitHub App name: Enter a unique name identifying your GitHub App, such as authenticating-with-rhdh-<GUID>.
   - Homepage URL: Enter your Developer Hub URL: https://<my_developer_hub_domain>.
   - Authorization callback URL: Enter your Developer Hub authentication backend URL: https://<my_developer_hub_domain>/api/auth/github/handler/frame.
   - Webhook: Clear "Active".
   - Organization permissions: Enable Read-only access to Members.
   - Where can this GitHub App be installed?: Select Only on this account.

2. In the General -> Clients secrets section, click Generate a new client secret.

3. In the Install App tab, choose an account to install your GitHub App on.

4. Save the following values for the next step:
   - Client ID
   - Client secret

5. Add your GitHub credentials to Developer Hub by adding the following key/value pairs to your Developer Hub secrets.
   You can use these secrets in the Developer Hub configuration files by using their environment variable name.

   - GITHUB_CLIENT_ID: Enter the saved Client ID.
   - GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET: Enter the saved Client Secret.
   - GITHUB_URL: Enter the GitHub host domain: github.com.
   - GITHUB_ORG: Enter your GitHub organization name, such as <your_github_organization_name>.

6. Enable the GitHub catalog provider plugin in your dynamic-plugins.yaml file to import GitHub users and groups to the Developer Hub software catalog.

   ```yaml
   plugins:
     - package: './dynamic-plugins/dist/backstage-plugin-catalog-backend-module-github-org-dynamic'
       disabled: false
   ```

7. Enable provisioning GitHub users and groups to the Developer Hub software catalog by adding the GitHub catalog provider section to your app-config.yaml file:

   ```yaml
   catalog:
     providers:
       githubOrg:
         id: githuborg
         githubUrl: "${GITHUB_URL}"
         orgs: [ "${GITHUB_ORG}" ]
         schedule:
           frequency:
             minutes: 30
           initialDelay:
             seconds: 15
           timeout:
             minutes: 15
   ```

8. Enable the GitHub authentication provider, by adding the GitHub authentication provider section to your app-config.yaml file:

   ```yaml
   auth:
     environment: production
     providers:
       github:
         production:
           clientId: ${GITHUB_CLIENT_ID}
           clientSecret: ${GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET}
           disableIdentityResolution: true
   ```

   where:
   - clientId: Enter the configured secret variable name: ${GITHUB_CLIENT_ID}.
   - clientSecret: Enter the configured secret variable name: ${GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET}.
